pages.title: Projective spaces, unirational varieties
pages.html:
1 Projective spaces, unirational varieties

Proposition 1

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(X=\mathbf{P}_k^r\) the projective space of dimension \(r\) over \(k\). Then \(X\) is simply connected, i.e. \(\pi_1(X)=0\).

For \(r=0\), this is trivial. If \(r=1\), one must show that if \(X'\) is a non-empty connected étale covering of \(X=\mathbf{P}_k^1\), then \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\). The genus formula gives us here, if \(g\) and \(g'\) are the genera of \(X\) and \(X'\):

\[ 1-g' = d(1-g), \]

where \(d\) is the degree of \(X'\) over \(X\). Since \(g=0\), we thus have \(1-g'=d\), which requires \(d=1\) since \(g'\geqslant 0\), which proves \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\). When \(r\geqslant 2\), one proceeds by induction on \(r\), assuming that \(\mathbf{P}^{r'}\) is simply connected for \(r'<r\). Applying this to a hyperplane of \(\mathbf{P}^r\) and using (X 10), it follows indeed that \(\mathbf{P}^r\) is simply connected. Another proof: we have \(\pi_1(\mathbf{P}^1\times\cdots\times \mathbf{P}^1) = \pi_1(\mathbf{P}^1)\times\cdots \times \pi_1(\mathbf{P}^1)\) by virtue of (X 7), hence \((\mathbf{P}^1)^r\) is simply connected since \(\mathbf{P}^1\) is, hence \(\mathbf{P}^r\) is simply connected by virtue of the birational invariance of the fundamental group (X 4). This proof shows more generally:

Corollary 2

Let \(X\) be a proper and normal scheme over an algebraically closed field \(k\); if \(X\) is a rational variety, i.e. integral and its field of functions is a pure transcendental extension of \(k\), then \(X\) is simply connected.

This result applies in particular to Grassmannian varieties and more generally to the varieties \(G/H\), where \(G\) is a connected linear group over \(k\) and \(H\) an algebraic subgroup containing a Borel subgroup of \(G\).

Let us recall that one calls a variety unirational over \(k\) a proper and integral scheme over \(k\) whose field of functions \(K\) is contained in a pure transcendental extension \(K'\) of \(k\), finite over \(K\) (i.e. having the same transcendence degree over \(k\) as \(K\)), i.e. if there exists a dominant rational map \(f\colon\mathbf{P}_k^r\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), with \(r=\dim X\). If \(X\) is normal, one thus sees by the remarks preceding X 4 that for every connected étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\), with field \(L/K\), the algebra \(L\otimes_K K'\) over \(K'\) is un- ramified over the model \(\mathbf{P}^r\), hence completely split by virtue of 1 which shows that \(L\) is \(K\)-isomorphic to a sub-extension of \(K'/K\). This therefore proves, taking into account V 2:

Corollary 3

The fundamental group of a normal unirational variety over an algebraically closed field is finite.

(N.B. One will note that in the definition of “unirational”, one did not need \(K'/K\) to be finite).

Remarks 4

Of course, the results of this section are well known. On the other hand, J-P. Serre has shown [10] that when \(X\) is a smooth projective unirational variety over an algebraically closed field of characteristic zero, \(X\) is simply connected. His proof is transcendental in that it uses the Hodge symmetry theorem.

Added in 2003 (MR) Let us restrict ourselves to the case of an algebraically closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(p\geqslant 0\). In characteristic \(p>0\), the definition of \(k\)-unirational variety given above is that of \(k\)-variety weakly unirational, as opposed to that of \(k\)-variety strongly unirational, where one moreover assumes that \(K'\) is a separable extension of \(K\).

In the case of dimension \(2\), there exist projective and smooth surfaces, weakly unirational, which have a non-trivial fundamental group (finite by 1.3) and which are therefore not rational (T. Shioda, On unirationality of supersingular surfaces, Math. Ann. 225 (1977), p. 155–159.). On the other hand a strongly unirational surface is rational: this follows from Castelnuovo's rationality criterion, extended to characteristic \(p>0\) by O. Zariski (cf. J-P. Serre, Sém. Bourbaki 146, 1957 and Œuvres–Collected papers, vol. 1, p. 467).

Over the field \(\mathbf{C}\) of complex numbers, one knows examples of projective and smooth varieties of dimension \(\geqslant 3\) which are unirational and non- rational (cf. P. Deligne, Variétés unirationnelles non rationnelles [d'après M. Artin et D. Mumford], Sém. Bourbaki 402, 1971-72, Lecture Notes vol. 317). This is the case in particular of a smooth cubic hypersurface in the projective space \(\mathbf{P}^4\). Some of these examples extend to characteristic \(>0\) to give strongly unirational non-rational varieties (cf. J.P. Murre, Reduction of the proof of the non-rationality of a non singular cubic threefold to a result of Mumford, Compositio Math. 27 (1973), p. 63–82).

Let \(V\) be a normal, integral projective \(k\)-variety. One says that \(V\) is rationally connected, if there exist an integral \(k\)-scheme of finite type \(T\) and a \(k\)-morphism \(F : T\times\mathbf{P}^1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) such that the morphism:

\[ \tag*{(1)} \begin{split} T \times\mathbf{P}^1\times\mathbf{P}^1&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\times V\\ (t, u, u')&\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}(F(t,u), F(t,u')) \end{split} \]

is dominant. In particular, through two sufficiently general rational points of \(V\) passes a rational curve. The terminology is justified by the fact that if \(V\) is rationally connected, one can join two rational points by a finite chain of rational curves. If \(k\) is of characteristic \(p>0\), one strengthens the preceding definition by requiring that the map (1) be generically smooth. One then obtains the notion of separably rationally connected variety. Thus unirational varieties are rationally connected and in characteristic \(p>0\), strongly unirational varieties are separably rationally connected. J. Kollár has shown that separably rationally connected varieties have a trivial algebraic fundamental group (cf. O. Debarre, Variétés rationnellement connexes [d'après T. Graber, J. Harris, J. Starr et A.J. de Jong], Sém. Bourbaki 906, 2001-2002).

pages.title: Abelian varieties
pages.html:
2 Abelian varieties

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(A\) an abelian variety over \(k\), i.e. a group scheme over \(k\), proper over \(k\), smooth over \(k\), and connected, and finally \(G\) a commutative group scheme of finite type over \(k\). Let us denote by \(\operatorname{Ext}(A,G)\) the group of classes of commutative extensions of \(A\) by \(G\), by \(\mathrm{H}^1(A,G)\) the group of classes of principal bundles on \(A\) with group \(G\) (compare 4 below), and let us consider the canonical homomorphism

\[ \operatorname{Ext}(A,G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(A,G). \]

An argument of Serre [5] shows that it is an injective homomorphism, whose image is the set of “primitive elements” of \(\mathrm{H}^1(A,G)\), i.e. of the elements \(\xi\) for which one has:

\[ \pi^*(\xi) = \mathrm{pr}_1^*(\xi) + \mathrm{pr}_2^*(\xi), \]

where \(\mathrm{pr}_i\) are the two projections of \(A\times A\) onto \(A\), and \(\pi\colon A\times A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) the composition law of \(A\) (N.B. Serre states his theorem only for \(G\) linear and connected, and of course smooth over \(k\), but by simplifying the first part of his argument, one sees that these restrictions are unnecessary: it suffices to note that every morphism of \(A\) into a group scheme \(E\) of finite type over \(k\), which carries the unit to the unit, is a group homomorphism, and to apply this to the sections over \(A\) of an extension \(E\) of \(A\) by \(G\)).

We shall apply this result to the case where \(G\) is a finite separable group over \(k\), i.e. an ordinary finite group, assumed commutative. Using then \(\pi_1(A\times A)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \pi_1(A)\times \pi_1(A)\) (X 7) and interpreting \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,G)\) as \(\operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1(X),G)\) for every algebraic scheme \(X\), in particular for \(X=A\) or \(X=A\times A\), one sees that every class of \(\mathrm{H}^1(A,G)\) is primitive, hence one has an isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{Ext}(A,G)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(A,G), \]

in other words every principal covering of \(A\) with commutative structural group \(G\), pointed above the origin of \(A\), is endowed in a unique way with a structure of algebraic group admitting the marked point as origin, and such that \(A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) is a homomorphism of algebraic groups. In particular, if \(A'\) is connected, it is likewise an abelian variety, isogenous to \(A\).

On the other hand, since the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \pi_1(X)\) from pointed algebraic schemes \(X\) into groups commutes with products (IX 7), it transforms a group in the first category into a group in the category of groups, i.e. into a commutative group. Hence if \(A\) is an abelian variety, \(\pi_1(A)\) is a commutative group. Hence in order to know \(\pi_1(A)\), it suffices to know the functor \(G\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathrm{H}^1(A,G)=\operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1(A),G)\) for \(G\) varying among the finite commutative groups. Finally, let us recall that for every integer \(n>0\), the multiplication-by-\(n\) homomorphism in \(A\):

\[ A\xrightarrow{n} A \]

is surjective, hence has finite kernel, i.e. it is an isogeny, and that it follows that every isogeny \(A'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) is a quotient of an isogeny of the preceding type. From this, and from standard arguments (cf. for example [6]) one draws:

Theorem 1 (Serre-Lang)

Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and for every integer \(n>0\) let us consider the ordinary finite group \(K_n\) underlying the kernel \({}_nA\) of multiplication by \(n\) in \(A\), and finally let us set for every prime number \(\ell\):

\[ T_\ell(A)=\varprojlim_r K_{\ell^r} \]

and

\[ T_{.}(A)=\underset{\ell}{\prod}T_\ell(A)=\varprojlim_n K_n \]

(where for \(m\) a multiple of \(n\), \(m=ns\), one sends \(K_m\) into \(K_n\) by multiplication by \(s\)). Then the group \(\pi_1(A)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(T_{.}(A)\), hence for every prime number \(\ell\), the \(\ell\)-primary component of \(\pi_1(A)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(T_\ell(A)\).

One will note that these isomorphisms are functorial for \(A\) variable. The module \(T_\ell(A)\) is called the \(\ell\)-adic Tate module of the abelian variety \(A\). It is an additive functor in \(A\), in particular it gives rise to a representation of the ring \(\operatorname{Hom}(A,A)\) of endomorphisms of \(A\) in \(T_\ell(A)\), called the \(\ell\)-adic representation of Weil, and which plays an important role in the theory of abelian varieties (cf. for example [4]). Theorem 1 gives an interpretation of it in terms of the natural representation in the \(\ell\)-adic homology group of \(A\), \(\mathrm{H}_1(A,\mathbf{Z}_\ell)=\pi_1(A)_\ell\), which is obviously more satisfactory a priori, notably from the point of view of the Lefschetz formula, [4]. Let us recall here the results of Weil on the structure of \(T_\ell(A)\):

  1. a)

    If \(n\) is prime to \(\operatorname{car}(k)\), then \(K_n\) is a free module of rank equal to \(2\dim A\) over \(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\), hence if \(\ell\) is a prime number \(\neq \operatorname{car}(k)\), \(T_\ell(A)\) is a free module of rank equal to \(2\dim A\) over the ring \(\mathbf{Z}_\ell\) of \(\ell\)-adic integers;

  2. b)

    If \(n\) is a power of \(\operatorname{car}(k)=p\), then \(K_n\) is a free module of rank \(\nu\leqslant\dim A\) over \(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\), \(\nu\) independent of \(n\), hence \(T_p(A)\) is a free module of rank \(\nu\leqslant \dim A\) over the ring \(\mathbf{Z}_p\) of \(p\)-adic integers.

This shows that in the theory of the fundamental group developed here, the fundamental group of a variable abelian variety does not vary in a regular manner with the parameter, its \(p\)-primary component being able to decrease abruptly for values of the parameter \(t\) corresponding to a residual characteristic \(p\); the best-known case of this phenomenon is that of elliptic curves. One will note however that, whatever \(n\) may be (prime or not to the characteristic) the true kernel \({}_nA\) in \(A\) for multiplication by \(n\) is a finite group scheme over \(k\) of degree \(n^{2g}\), where \(g=\dim A\), which will be non-separable over \(k\) if \(n\) is a multiple of \(p=\operatorname{car}(k)\). Moreover, when \(A\) varies in a family of abelian varieties, i.e. if one has an abelian scheme \(A\) over a base scheme \(S\), one shows more generally that \({}_n A\) is a finite and flat group scheme over \(S\), of degree \(n^{2g}\) over \(S\), that is to say, on condition of taking into account the infinitesimal parts of the kernels \({}_n A\), they behave in a regular manner whatever \(n\) may be. This suggests that the “true” fundamental group of an abelian variety \(A\) is the pro-algebraic-group (formal inverse limit of finite groups over \(k\)) \(\varprojlim_n{{}_nA}\), where by “true fundamental group” of an algebraic scheme \(X\), one must understand: the pro-group which classifies the principal coverings of \(X\) with structural group an arbitrary finite group \(G\) over \(k\) (not necessarily separable over \(k\)). In this way for example, one recovers, through the representations of \(\operatorname{Hom}(A,A)\) in the \(p\)-primary component of the true fundamental group of \(A\), the characteristic polynomial of Weil defined by the latter by means of the \(\ell\neq p\), in a more natural way than the construction of Serre [8].

This suggests that the “true” fundamental group of an abelian variety \(A\) is the pro-algebraic-group (formal inverse limit of finite groups over \(k\)) \(\varprojlim_n{{}_nA}\), where by “true fundamental group” of an algebraic scheme \(X\), one must understand: the pro-group which classifies the principal coverings of \(X\) with structural group an arbitrary finite group \(G\) over \(k\) (not necessarily separable over \(k\)). In this way for example, one recovers, through the representations of \(\operatorname{Hom}(A,A)\) in the \(p\)-primary component of the true fundamental group of \(A\), the characteristic polynomial of Weil defined by the latter by means of the \(\ell\neq p\), in a more natural way than the construction of Serre [8].

pages.title: Projecting cones, Zariski's example
pages.html:
3 Projecting cones, Zariski's example

Let \(k\) again be an algebraically closed field, for simplicity, and let \(V\) be a connected projective \(k\)-scheme, a closed subscheme of \(\mathbf{P}^r_k\), which one may if one wishes assume non singular. Let \(Y=\widehat{C}\) be the projective projecting cone of \(V\), \(y_0\) its vertex, \(X=\widehat{C}_V\) the usual projective closure of the vector bundle \(C_V=\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{O}_V(1))\) associated with \(\mathscr{O}_V(1)\), and finally

\[ f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y \]

the canonical morphism, contracting the zero section \(X_0\) of \(C_V\) onto \(X\) to a point (EGA II 8.6.4). Since \(X\) is a locally trivial bundle over \(V\), with fibres \(\mathbf{P}^1\) and hence with simply connected fibres, the morphism \(p\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) induces, by virtue of (X 4), an isomorphism:

\[ \pi_1(X)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\pi_1(V). \]

Since \(p\) induces an isomorphism \(X_0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\), we conclude that an étale covering of \(X\) is completely decomposed if and only if its restriction to \(X_0\) is so. Now for every étale covering \(Y'\) of \(Y\), the inverse image \(X'=X\times_Y Y'\) is an étale covering of \(X\) that is completely decomposed over the fibre \(X_0\), hence trivial. Since the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(Y)\) is surjective (IX 4), we conclude that

\[ \pi_1(Y)=(e) \]

in other words every projective projecting cone is simply connected. (N.B. in characteristic \(0\), the same result will hold on taking for \(Y\) the affine projecting cone).

Suppose now that \(V\) is regular, i.e. smooth over \(k\); then \(X\) is regular, and for a suitable projective immersion of \(V\), one then finds a projecting cone \(Y\) that is normal. If \(V\) is not simply connected, hence \(X\) not simply connected, let \(X'\) be a nontrivial connected étale covering of \(X\). Since the fibres of \(X\) at the points \(y\in Y\) distinct from \(y_0\) are reduced to a point, one sees that the restriction of \(X'\) to its fibres (in particular to the generic fibre) is trivial; however \(X\) does not arise by inverse image from an étale covering of \(Y\), since \(Y\) is simply connected and \(X'\) would be completely decomposed. This shows that (X 3 and 4) become false if one replaces the hypothesis that \(f\) is separable by the weaker one that its fibres are separable algebraic schemes (or even smooth ones) over the \(\mathit{k}(s)\). One notes likewise that the fundamental groups of the geometric fibres \(\overline X_y\) of the \(y\neq y_0\) are obviously reduced to \((e)\) since these fibres are reduced to a point, whereas \(\pi_1(X_0)\neq e\), so the semicontinuity theorem (X 4) also fails for \(f\).

Let us finally indicate the example, pointed out by Zariski, that refutes these same theorems when one replaces in them the hypothesis that \(f\) is separable by the one that \(f\) is flat. Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism from a non singular projective surface into the rational line \(Y=\mathbf{P}^1\), such that \(K=k(X)\) is a “regular” extension, i.e. primary and separable, of \(k(f)\), (i.e. the geometric generic fibre is connected and separable), and such that the divisor \((f)=X_0-X_\infty\) is a multiple of a divisor (where \(n\) is an integer prime to the characteristic). It is possible to construct such examples in every characteristic. Let \(X'\) be the normalization of \(X\) in \(K(f^{1/n})\), where \(K=k(X)\) is the function field of \(X\). It follows from the hypothesis on \((f)\) that \(X'\) is étale over \(X\). Let \(Y'\) be the normalization of \(Y\) in \(k(t)(t^{1/n})\); it is ramified over \(Y\) at the points \(t=0\) and \(t=\infty\) exactly, and the restriction \(X'|f^{-1}(U)\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of \(Y'|U\). In particular, the restriction of \(X'\) to the geometric generic fibre of \(X\) over \(Y\) decomposes completely. However, \(X'\) is not isomorphic to the inverse image of an étale covering of \(Y\), for one sees at once that the latter would necessarily be \(Y'\), which is absurd since \(Y'\) is ramified over \(Y\)1.

Here (after Serre) is a simple way to realize the conditions of this example, taking inspiration from [5]: one takes for \(n\) a prime number \(\geqslant 5\), distinct from the characteristic, and one makes \(G=\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\) act on \(\underline{k}^4\)2 by multiplying the coordinates by four distinct characters of \(G\) (which is possible since \(n\geqslant 5\)). Then \(G\) acts on the projective space \(\mathbf{P}^3_k\), and the only fixed points under \(G\) are the four points corresponding to the coordinate axes. The surface \(X'\) of equation \(x^n+y^n+z^n+t^n=0\) is smooth over \(k\) (Jacobian criterion), and contains none of the fixed points, so \(G\), being of prime order, acts on \(X\) “without fixed points”, i.e. \(X\) is a principal covering of \(X=X'/G\) with group \(G\). Let \(g=x/y\) in \(k(X')=K'\); it is a Kummer generator of \(K'\) over \(K=k(X)\) if the chosen characters were \(\chi^i\), \(i=0,1,2,3\), with \(\chi\) a primitive character, let \(f\) be its power, which is an element of \(K\). One sees at once that \(K'\) is a regular extension of \(k(g)\), which results from the fact that the plane curve of homogeneous equation in \(U,T,Z\): \(T^n+Z^n+(1+g^n)U^n=0\) is smooth over \(k(g)\) (Jacobian criterion), and that one knows that every plane curve is connected. On the other hand one has \(k(f)=K\cap k(g)\), since the right-hand side is an extension of \(k(f)\) contained in the extension \(k(g)\) of prime degree, and distinct from \(k(g)\) (since \(g\not\in K\)). This implies that \(K\) is a regular extension of \(k(f)\). Finally the divisor of \(f\) on \(X\) is a multiple of a divisor, for its inverse image on \(X'\) is the divisor of \(g^n\), hence a multiple, and one can descend because \(X'\) is étale over \(X\). One would be done if the rational map \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}^1\) were a morphism, that is to say if the divisors of the zeros and of the poles of \(f\) did not meet. In fact, one verifies easily (by looking again on \(X'\)) that the two divisors in question are the products by \(n\) of two curves smooth over \(k\), meeting transversally at a point \(a\). Replacing now \(X\) by the scheme obtained by blowing up \(a\), the preceding conditions (\(\operatorname{div}(f)\) divisible by \(n\), and \(k(X_1)=k(X)\) a regular extension of \(k(X)\)) remain satisfied, but moreover \(f\) is a morphism \(X_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}^1\), so we are under the desired conditions.
