pages.title: The exact cohomology sequence
pages.html:
4 The exact cohomology sequence

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, so that the category \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) of preschemes over \(X\) is determined, hence also the notion of group in the latter, which one will also call a group prescheme over \(S\), or simply an \(S\)-group. To simplify the exposition and to fix ideas, we shall for the most part in what follows restrict ourselves to groups which are affine and flat over \(S\)1, which will suffice for the applications we have in view. (Of course, one encounters numerous cases where neither hypothesis is satisfied). Let \(G\) be such an \(S\)-group, and let \(P\) be a prescheme over \(S\) on which \(G\) operates on the right, which implies in particular a morphism

\[ \pi:P\times_SG\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P \]

in the category \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\), satisfying the well-known axioms. One says that \(P\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G\) if the morphism

\[ P\times_S G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P\times_S P \]

with components \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\pi\) is an isomorphism; it amounts to the same to say that for every object \(S'\) of \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S'}\), \(P(S')=\operatorname{Hom}_S(S',P)\) considered as a set with group of operators \(G(S')=\operatorname{Hom}_S(S',G)\), is empty or principal homogeneous (i.e. empty or isomorphic to \(G(S')\) on which the group \(G(S')\) operates by right translations). One says that \(P\) is trivial if \(P\) is isomorphic to \(G\), on which \(G\) operates by right translations, or what amounts to the same, if each of the sets with operators \(P(S')\) under \(G(S')\) is trivial. One verifies, for example by the patented process of passing to the set-theoretic case, that \(P\) is trivial if and only if it is formally principal homogeneous, and admits a section over \(S\) (this last fact being stated in categorical terms by saying that \(P\) has a section over the final object \(e=S\) of \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\), i.e. that there exists a morphism from \(e\) to \(P\)). To define the notion of principal homogeneous bundle \(P\) under \(G\), stronger than that of formally principal homogeneous bundle, one must first specify in \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) a set of morphisms which will be used for the “descent”, and will play the role of “localization morphisms” for “trivializing” bundles. The most adequate choice varies according to the context, none containing all the others2. Here, it will be convenient to adopt the following definition:

Definition 1

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group. One calls a principal homogeneous bundle (on the right) under \(G\), an \(S\)-prescheme \(P\) with right \(S\)-group \(G\), such that there exists a covering of \(S\) by open sets \(U_i\), and for every \(i\) a base-change morphism \(S'_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U_i\) faithfully flat and quasi-compact, such that \(P'=P\times_S S'\) is a trivial prescheme with operators under \(G'=G\times_S S'\), where \(S'\) is the \(S\)-prescheme disjoint sum of the \(S'_i\).

(One will note that the base-change functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'=X\times_S S'\), being left exact, transforms groups into groups, objects with group of operators into objects with group of operators). Let us note that 1 is stable under base change. Let us also note:

Proposition 2

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group, flat and quasi-compact over \(S\), \(P\) an \(S\)-prescheme where \(G\) operates on the right. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(P\) is a principal homogeneous bundle under \(G\);

  2. (ii)

    \(P\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G\), and the structural morphism \(P\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact.

If \(P\) is principal homogeneous under \(G\), then with the notations of 1 \(P'\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(S'\) (since \(G'\) is, and \(P'\) is \(S'\)-isomorphic to it), hence \(P\) has the same properties over \(S\), (for “surjective” and “quasi-compact”, cf. VIII 1, for “flat” it is an omission in the sorites of Exposé VIII). Conversely, if (ii) is satisfied, let us take the base change \(S'=P\), which is indeed faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(S\); then \(P'\) will be formally principal homogeneous over \(S'\) since \(P\) is so over \(S\) and since the base-change functor is left exact, on the other hand \(P'\) has a section over \(S'\), namely the diagonal section, hence it is a trivial principal bundle, which completes the proof.

Corollary 3

If \(G\) is affine and flat over \(S\), every principal homogeneous bundle \(P\) under \(G\) is affine and flat over \(S\).

Indeed, it becomes so by faithfully flat and quasi-compact extension of the base, and one applies (VIII 6).

The usefulness of Definition 1 for \(S\)-groups flat and affine over \(S\) rests on (VIII 1), i.e. on the fact that the morphisms \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) envisaged in 1 are morphisms of effective descent for the category of preschemes affine over others. Thanks to this fact, the verification of the facts sketched below is carried out in an essentially “categorical” manner3. Let \(E\) be an \(S\)-prescheme on which the \(S\)-group \(G\) operates on the left, and let \(P\) be a principal homogeneous bundle (on the right) under \(G\), we wish to define an associated bundle \(E^{(P)}\), “locally” isomorphic to \(E\). For this, let us make operate on the right \(G\) in \(P\times_S E\) according to the law \((x,y)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} (xg,g^{-1}y)\), which describes such operations in the set-theoretic context, and extends to categories by the patented process. One will set, subject to existence:

\[ E^{(P)}=(P\times_S E)/G \]

whereupon one observes that \(P\times_S E\) will be a prescheme over \(T=E^{(P)}\), with right group of operators \(G_T=G\times_S T\); to be comfortable, one would like moreover that \(P\times_S E\) be a principal homogeneous bundle over \(T\) with group \(G_T\). To verify the existence of \(E^{(P)}\) and the preceding property, let us take again the \(S'\) of Definition 1 and look at the inverse-image situation over \(S'\) of the initial situation. From the fact that \(P'\) is trivial, i.e. isomorphic to \(G'_d\), one sees immediately that \(E^{\prime(P')}\) exists, and has the desired exactness property. In fact, \(E'\times_{S'} P'\) is \(G'\)-isomorphic to the product \(E'\times_{S'} G'\), hence \(E^{\prime(P')}\) is isomorphic to \(E'\). Moreover, the formation of the “associated bundle” in the case of a trivial space with operators commutes with every extension of the base, and taking in this instance the various base extensions \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{S''}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{S'}}\) and \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{S'''}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar@<0pt>[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{S'}}\), where \(S''\) and \(S'''\) are the double and triple fibre products of \(S'\) over \(S\), one observes that \(E^{\prime(P')}\) is equipped with a descent datum relative to the morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and that \(E^{(P)}\) exists with the required properties if and only if this descent datum is effective; of course \(E^{(P)}\) is then none other than the descended object. (Use the fact that \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a descent morphism in the category of \(S\)-preschemes, cf. VIII 2). It follows that the associated bundle exists if \(E\) is affine over \(S\). We shall apply this construction to the case where one has a homomorphism of \(S\)-groups \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\), and where one takes for \(E\) the \(S\)-prescheme \(H\) equipped with the operations of \(G\) on \(H\) on the left resulting from the given morphism; since \(H\) operates on the right on itself in such a way as to commute with the operations of \(G\) on \(H\), and since (subject to existence over \(S\)) the formation of the associated bundle commutes with extension of the base, one observes easily that \(H\) will operate on the right on \(P^{(H)}\), which is thereupon a principal homogeneous bundle under \(H\) in the sense of 1, and precisely is trivialized by the same morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) as \(P\). In particular, to every principal homogeneous bundle \(P\) under \(G\) and every homomorphism of \(S\)-groups \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\), with \(H\) affine over \(S\), is associated a principal homogeneous bundle with group \(H\), functorially in \((G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H)\), and compatibly with arbitrary base changes \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\).

Definition 4

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-prescheme. We denote by \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,G)\) the set of sections of \(G\) over \(S\), which we shall regard as a group whenever \(G\) is an \(S\)-group. In this case, we denote by \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of principal homogeneous bundles over \(S\) with group \(S\), regarding \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) as endowed with the “marked point” corresponding to the trivial bundles4.

Thus, \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,G)\) is a functor in the \(S\)-prescheme \(G\), with values in the category of sets. This functor is left exact, a fortiori it commutes with finite products, which indeed implies that it transforms groups into groups, commutative groups into commutative groups. In an analogous way, \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) is a functor in the affine \(S\)-group \(G\), with values in the category of sets by virtue of the formation of associated bundles; one readily verifies that this functor commutes with finite products. In particular it transforms the groups in the category of affine \(S\)-groups, i.e. the commutative affine \(S\)-groups, into groups of the second one, and even into commutative groups (since the groups of the first category are commutative). Thus, if \(G\) is a commutative affine \(S\)-group, \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) is a commutative group, and a homomorphism \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\) of commutative affine \(S\)-groups gives rise to a homomorphism of groups \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,H)\).

To simplify, we restrict ourselves in what follows to the consideration of affine and commutative \(S\)-groups. Let

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G'\xrightarrow{u} G\xrightarrow{v}G''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

be a sequence of morphisms of such groups; we shall say that this sequence is exact if \(vu=0\) (which permits one to regard \(G\) as a prescheme over \(G''\), with right operator group \(G'_{G''}\)) and if \(G\) is a principal homogeneous bundle over \(G''\) with group \(G'_{G''}=G'\times_S G''\). This implies in particular that \(u\colon G'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) is a kernel of \(v\), and a fortiori it implies the exactness of the sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G''). \]

It moreover implies the possibility of defining a map

\[ \partial\colon\mathrm{H}^0(X,G'')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G'), \]

by associating to every section of \(G''\) over \(S\), i.e. to every \(S\)-morphism \(f\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G''\), the principal homogeneous bundle \(P_f\) with group \(G'\simeq f^*(G'_{G''})\) over \(S\), the inverse image of the principal homogeneous bundle \(G\) over \(G''\). From the point of view of \(S\)-preschemes, it is therefore nothing other than the inverse image under \(v\colon G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G''\) of the image sub-prescheme of \(S\) under the immersion \(f\), and the operations of \(G'\) on \(P_f\) are induced by the right operations of \(G'\) on \(G\). We likewise leave to the reader the verification of the following proposition, which presents no difficulties other than of exposition:

Proposition 5

The map \(\partial\colon \mathrm{H}^0(X,G'')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G')\) is a homomorphism of groups. The following sequence of homomorphisms is exact:

\begin{multline*} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G')\xrightarrow{u^0}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G)\xrightarrow{v^0}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G')\xrightarrow{\partial}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G'')\\ \xrightarrow{u^1}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G) \xrightarrow{v^1}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G'') \end{multline*}

(where the homomorphisms other than \(\partial\) come from the functorial law of \(\mathrm{H}^0\) resp. \(\mathrm{H}^1\)).

Remarks 6

The point of view expounded here for the study of principal homogeneous bundles is visibly inspired by Serre [7], which the reader will have every interest in consulting. When one wishes a formalism that applies equally to structural \(S\)-groups which are quasi-projective over \(S\) (so as to encompass the projective abelian schemes in particular), it is advantageous to modify 1 by requiring therein that \(S'\) be a sum of preschemes \(S'_i\) which are finite and locally free over open sets \(S_i\) of \(S\) covering \(S\). The foregoing developments are then valid, including in particular 5, upon replacing everywhere the hypothesis of affineness by that of quasi-projectivity, and by interpreting in a corresponding way the definition given above of an exact sequence of \(S\)-groups. Indeed, it suffices to replace the reference to (VIII 1) by (VIII 7): the morphisms used \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) are morphisms of effective descent for the fibred category of quasi-projective preschemes over others. One should take care, however, that this second notion of principal homogeneous bundle is more restrictive than the first 1.

7

One obtains an even more restrictive notion of principal homogeneous bundle by requiring that \(S\) be covered by open sets \(S_i\) such that for every \(i\), \(P|S_i\) is a trivial bundle with operators under \(G|S_i\): one then says that \(P\) is a locally trivial principal homogeneous bundle. The classes of these bundles, for given \(G\), form a subset of \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,G)\), which is in one-to-one correspondence with \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,\mathscr{O}_X(G))\), where \(\mathscr{O}_X(G)\) is the sheaf (in the ordinary sense) of sections of \(G\) over \(S\), cf. [2]. In order that these \(\mathrm{H}^1\) still give rise to an exact sequence of cohomology 5, one must evidently assume that the sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) is exact in the sense that is reasonable for this new context, i.e. that \(G\) is a locally trivial bundle over \(G''\), with group \(G'_{G''}\); this also means that \(u\colon G'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) is a kernel of \(v\colon G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G''\), and that \(G\) admits locally a section over \(G''\).

8

It is evidently very desirable to continue the exact sequence 5 by introducing the higher cohomology groups \(\mathrm{H}^i(X,G)\). This is possible by placing oneself within the framework of “Weil Cohomology”: one considers the category \(\mathscr{B}\) of quasi-compact preschemes over \(S\), equipped with the set \(\mathscr{M}\) of faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphisms, which we shall call localizing morphisms. An abelian “Weil sheaf” over \(S\) (or better, over \((\mathscr{B},\mathscr{M})\)) is then a contravariant functor \(\mathscr{F}\) from \(\mathscr{B}\) to the category of abelian groups, transforming sums into products, and a sequence \({\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{T''=T'\times_TT'}\ar@<+2pt>[rr]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1,\mathrm{pr}_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{T'}\ar[r]^f&{T}}}\), with \(f\in\mathscr{M}\), into an exact diagram of sets \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathscr{F}(T)}\ar[r]&{\mathscr{F}(T')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathscr{F}(T'')}}\). The Weil sheaves form an abelian category with exact direct limits admitting a generator, hence admitting enough injective objects [1]. The right derived functors of the functor \(\Gamma(\mathscr{F})=\mathscr{F}(S)\) are then denoted \(\mathrm{H}^i(S,\mathscr{F})\). On the other hand, every commutative \(S\)-group evidently defines a Weil sheaf (VIII 2), whose \(\mathrm{H}^0\) and \(\mathrm{H}^1\) are none other than \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,G)\) and \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\), which permits one to define the other \(\mathrm{H}^i(S,G)\) in a reasonable way. One moreover shows that an exact sequence of \(S\)-groups defines an exact sequence of Weil sheaves, which permits one to recover and to prolong the exact sequence 55.

9

It would be appropriate to develop the non-commutative variants of 5 as in [2]. For a systematic development, in the appropriate framework, of the various cohomological notions sketched in the present number, we refer to a work in preparation by J. Giraud6.

blocks.html: 4 The exact cohomology sequence

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, so that the category \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) of preschemes over \(X\) is determined, hence also the notion of group in the latter, which one will also call a group prescheme over \(S\), or simply an \(S\)-group. To simplify the exposition and to fix ideas, we shall for the most part in what follows restrict ourselves to groups which are affine and flat over \(S\)1, which will suffice for the applications we have in view. (Of course, one encounters numerous cases where neither hypothesis is satisfied). Let \(G\) be such an \(S\)-group, and let \(P\) be a prescheme over \(S\) on which \(G\) operates on the right, which implies in particular a morphism
\[ \pi:P\times_SG\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P \]
in the category \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\), satisfying the well-known axioms. One says that \(P\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G\) if the morphism
\[ P\times_S G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P\times_S P \]

with components \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\pi\) is an isomorphism; it amounts to the same to say that for every object \(S'\) of \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S'}\), \(P(S')=\operatorname{Hom}_S(S',P)\) considered as a set with group of operators \(G(S')=\operatorname{Hom}_S(S',G)\), is empty or principal homogeneous (i.e. empty or isomorphic to \(G(S')\) on which the group \(G(S')\) operates by right translations). One says that \(P\) is trivial if \(P\) is isomorphic to \(G\), on which \(G\) operates by right translations, or what amounts to the same, if each of the sets with operators \(P(S')\) under \(G(S')\) is trivial. One verifies, for example by the patented process of passing to the set-theoretic case, that \(P\) is trivial if and only if it is formally principal homogeneous, and admits a section over \(S\) (this last fact being stated in categorical terms by saying that \(P\) has a section over the final object \(e=S\) of \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\), i.e. that there exists a morphism from \(e\) to \(P\)). To define the notion of principal homogeneous bundle \(P\) under \(G\), stronger than that of formally principal homogeneous bundle, one must first specify in \(\mathbf{Sch}_{/S}\) a set of morphisms which will be used for the “descent”, and will play the role of “localization morphisms” for “trivializing” bundles. The most adequate choice varies according to the context, none containing all the others2. Here, it will be convenient to adopt the following definition:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group. One calls a principal homogeneous bundle (on the right) under \(G\), an \(S\)-prescheme \(P\) with right \(S\)-group \(G\), such that there exists a covering of \(S\) by open sets \(U_i\), and for every \(i\) a base-change morphism \(S'_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U_i\) faithfully flat and quasi-compact, such that \(P'=P\times_S S'\) is a trivial prescheme with operators under \(G'=G\times_S S'\), where \(S'\) is the \(S\)-prescheme disjoint sum of the \(S'_i\).

(One will note that the base-change functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'=X\times_S S'\), being left exact, transforms groups into groups, objects with group of operators into objects with group of operators). Let us note that 1 is stable under base change. Let us also note:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-group, flat and quasi-compact over \(S\), \(P\) an \(S\)-prescheme where \(G\) operates on the right. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(P\) is a principal homogeneous bundle under \(G\);

  2. (ii)

    \(P\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G\), and the structural morphism \(P\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact.

If \(P\) is principal homogeneous under \(G\), then with the notations of 1 \(P'\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(S'\) (since \(G'\) is, and \(P'\) is \(S'\)-isomorphic to it), hence \(P\) has the same properties over \(S\), (for “surjective” and “quasi-compact”, cf. VIII 1, for “flat” it is an omission in the sorites of Exposé VIII). Conversely, if (ii) is satisfied, let us take the base change \(S'=P\), which is indeed faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(S\); then \(P'\) will be formally principal homogeneous over \(S'\) since \(P\) is so over \(S\) and since the base-change functor is left exact, on the other hand \(P'\) has a section over \(S'\), namely the diagonal section, hence it is a trivial principal bundle, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

If \(G\) is affine and flat over \(S\), every principal homogeneous bundle \(P\) under \(G\) is affine and flat over \(S\).
Indeed, it becomes so by faithfully flat and quasi-compact extension of the base, and one applies (VIII 6).

The usefulness of Definition 1 for \(S\)-groups flat and affine over \(S\) rests on (VIII 1), i.e. on the fact that the morphisms \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) envisaged in 1 are morphisms of effective descent for the category of preschemes affine over others. Thanks to this fact, the verification of the facts sketched below is carried out in an essentially “categorical” manner3. Let \(E\) be an \(S\)-prescheme on which the \(S\)-group \(G\) operates on the left, and let \(P\) be a principal homogeneous bundle (on the right) under \(G\), we wish to define an associated bundle \(E^{(P)}\), “locally” isomorphic to \(E\). For this, let us make operate on the right \(G\) in \(P\times_S E\) according to the law \((x,y)\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} (xg,g^{-1}y)\), which describes such operations in the set-theoretic context, and extends to categories by the patented process. One will set, subject to existence:
\[ E^{(P)}=(P\times_S E)/G \]

whereupon one observes that \(P\times_S E\) will be a prescheme over \(T=E^{(P)}\), with right group of operators \(G_T=G\times_S T\); to be comfortable, one would like moreover that \(P\times_S E\) be a principal homogeneous bundle over \(T\) with group \(G_T\). To verify the existence of \(E^{(P)}\) and the preceding property, let us take again the \(S'\) of Definition 1 and look at the inverse-image situation over \(S'\) of the initial situation. From the fact that \(P'\) is trivial, i.e. isomorphic to \(G'_d\), one sees immediately that \(E^{\prime(P')}\) exists, and has the desired exactness property. In fact, \(E'\times_{S'} P'\) is \(G'\)-isomorphic to the product \(E'\times_{S'} G'\), hence \(E^{\prime(P')}\) is isomorphic to \(E'\). Moreover, the formation of the “associated bundle” in the case of a trivial space with operators commutes with every extension of the base, and taking in this instance the various base extensions \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{S''}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{S'}}\) and \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{S'''}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar@<0pt>[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{S'}}\), where \(S''\) and \(S'''\) are the double and triple fibre products of \(S'\) over \(S\), one observes that \(E^{\prime(P')}\) is equipped with a descent datum relative to the morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), and that \(E^{(P)}\) exists with the required properties if and only if this descent datum is effective; of course \(E^{(P)}\) is then none other than the descended object. (Use the fact that \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a descent morphism in the category of \(S\)-preschemes, cf. VIII 2). It follows that the associated bundle exists if \(E\) is affine over \(S\). We shall apply this construction to the case where one has a homomorphism of \(S\)-groups \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\), and where one takes for \(E\) the \(S\)-prescheme \(H\) equipped with the operations of \(G\) on \(H\) on the left resulting from the given morphism; since \(H\) operates on the right on itself in such a way as to commute with the operations of \(G\) on \(H\), and since (subject to existence over \(S\)) the formation of the associated bundle commutes with extension of the base, one observes easily that \(H\) will operate on the right on \(P^{(H)}\), which is thereupon a principal homogeneous bundle under \(H\) in the sense of 1, and precisely is trivialized by the same morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) as \(P\). In particular, to every principal homogeneous bundle \(P\) under \(G\) and every homomorphism of \(S\)-groups \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\), with \(H\) affine over \(S\), is associated a principal homogeneous bundle with group \(H\), functorially in \((G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H)\), and compatibly with arbitrary base changes \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4

Let \(G\) be an \(S\)-prescheme. We denote by \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,G)\) the set of sections of \(G\) over \(S\), which we shall regard as a group whenever \(G\) is an \(S\)-group. In this case, we denote by \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of principal homogeneous bundles over \(S\) with group \(S\), regarding \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) as endowed with the “marked point” corresponding to the trivial bundles4.

Thus, \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,G)\) is a functor in the \(S\)-prescheme \(G\), with values in the category of sets. This functor is left exact, a fortiori it commutes with finite products, which indeed implies that it transforms groups into groups, commutative groups into commutative groups. In an analogous way, \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) is a functor in the affine \(S\)-group \(G\), with values in the category of sets by virtue of the formation of associated bundles; one readily verifies that this functor commutes with finite products. In particular it transforms the groups in the category of affine \(S\)-groups, i.e. the commutative affine \(S\)-groups, into groups of the second one, and even into commutative groups (since the groups of the first category are commutative). Thus, if \(G\) is a commutative affine \(S\)-group, \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) is a commutative group, and a homomorphism \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\) of commutative affine \(S\)-groups gives rise to a homomorphism of groups \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,H)\).
To simplify, we restrict ourselves in what follows to the consideration of affine and commutative \(S\)-groups. Let
\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G'\xrightarrow{u} G\xrightarrow{v}G''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

be a sequence of morphisms of such groups; we shall say that this sequence is exact if \(vu=0\) (which permits one to regard \(G\) as a prescheme over \(G''\), with right operator group \(G'_{G''}\)) and if \(G\) is a principal homogeneous bundle over \(G''\) with group \(G'_{G''}=G'\times_S G''\). This implies in particular that \(u\colon G'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) is a kernel of \(v\), and a fortiori it implies the exactness of the sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G''). \]
It moreover implies the possibility of defining a map
\[ \partial\colon\mathrm{H}^0(X,G'')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G'), \]

by associating to every section of \(G''\) over \(S\), i.e. to every \(S\)-morphism \(f\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G''\), the principal homogeneous bundle \(P_f\) with group \(G'\simeq f^*(G'_{G''})\) over \(S\), the inverse image of the principal homogeneous bundle \(G\) over \(G''\). From the point of view of \(S\)-preschemes, it is therefore nothing other than the inverse image under \(v\colon G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G''\) of the image sub-prescheme of \(S\) under the immersion \(f\), and the operations of \(G'\) on \(P_f\) are induced by the right operations of \(G'\) on \(G\). We likewise leave to the reader the verification of the following proposition, which presents no difficulties other than of exposition:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5

The map \(\partial\colon \mathrm{H}^0(X,G'')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G')\) is a homomorphism of groups. The following sequence of homomorphisms is exact:

\begin{multline*} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G')\xrightarrow{u^0}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G)\xrightarrow{v^0}\mathrm{H}^0(X,G')\xrightarrow{\partial}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G'')\\ \xrightarrow{u^1}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G) \xrightarrow{v^1}\mathrm{H}^1(X,G'') \end{multline*}

(where the homomorphisms other than \(\partial\) come from the functorial law of \(\mathrm{H}^0\) resp. \(\mathrm{H}^1\)).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 6

The point of view expounded here for the study of principal homogeneous bundles is visibly inspired by Serre [7], which the reader will have every interest in consulting. When one wishes a formalism that applies equally to structural \(S\)-groups which are quasi-projective over \(S\) (so as to encompass the projective abelian schemes in particular), it is advantageous to modify 1 by requiring therein that \(S'\) be a sum of preschemes \(S'_i\) which are finite and locally free over open sets \(S_i\) of \(S\) covering \(S\). The foregoing developments are then valid, including in particular 5, upon replacing everywhere the hypothesis of affineness by that of quasi-projectivity, and by interpreting in a corresponding way the definition given above of an exact sequence of \(S\)-groups. Indeed, it suffices to replace the reference to (VIII 1) by (VIII 7): the morphisms used \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) are morphisms of effective descent for the fibred category of quasi-projective preschemes over others. One should take care, however, that this second notion of principal homogeneous bundle is more restrictive than the first 1.

One obtains an even more restrictive notion of principal homogeneous bundle by requiring that \(S\) be covered by open sets \(S_i\) such that for every \(i\), \(P|S_i\) is a trivial bundle with operators under \(G|S_i\): one then says that \(P\) is a locally trivial principal homogeneous bundle. The classes of these bundles, for given \(G\), form a subset of \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,G)\), which is in one-to-one correspondence with \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,\mathscr{O}_X(G))\), where \(\mathscr{O}_X(G)\) is the sheaf (in the ordinary sense) of sections of \(G\) over \(S\), cf. [2]. In order that these \(\mathrm{H}^1\) still give rise to an exact sequence of cohomology 5, one must evidently assume that the sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) is exact in the sense that is reasonable for this new context, i.e. that \(G\) is a locally trivial bundle over \(G''\), with group \(G'_{G''}\); this also means that \(u\colon G'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) is a kernel of \(v\colon G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G''\), and that \(G\) admits locally a section over \(G''\).

It is evidently very desirable to continue the exact sequence 5 by introducing the higher cohomology groups \(\mathrm{H}^i(X,G)\). This is possible by placing oneself within the framework of “Weil Cohomology”: one considers the category \(\mathscr{B}\) of quasi-compact preschemes over \(S\), equipped with the set \(\mathscr{M}\) of faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphisms, which we shall call localizing morphisms. An abelian “Weil sheaf” over \(S\) (or better, over \((\mathscr{B},\mathscr{M})\)) is then a contravariant functor \(\mathscr{F}\) from \(\mathscr{B}\) to the category of abelian groups, transforming sums into products, and a sequence \({\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{T''=T'\times_TT'}\ar@<+2pt>[rr]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1,\mathrm{pr}_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{T'}\ar[r]^f&{T}}}\), with \(f\in\mathscr{M}\), into an exact diagram of sets \(\xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\mathscr{F}(T)}\ar[r]&{\mathscr{F}(T')}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\mathscr{F}(T'')}}\). The Weil sheaves form an abelian category with exact direct limits admitting a generator, hence admitting enough injective objects [1]. The right derived functors of the functor \(\Gamma(\mathscr{F})=\mathscr{F}(S)\) are then denoted \(\mathrm{H}^i(S,\mathscr{F})\). On the other hand, every commutative \(S\)-group evidently defines a Weil sheaf (VIII 2), whose \(\mathrm{H}^0\) and \(\mathrm{H}^1\) are none other than \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,G)\) and \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\), which permits one to define the other \(\mathrm{H}^i(S,G)\) in a reasonable way. One moreover shows that an exact sequence of \(S\)-groups defines an exact sequence of Weil sheaves, which permits one to recover and to prolong the exact sequence 55.

It would be appropriate to develop the non-commutative variants of 5 as in [2]. For a systematic development, in the appropriate framework, of the various cohomological notions sketched in the present number, we refer to a work in preparation by J. Giraud6.

footnotes.html: In fact, for what follows, the hypothesis quasi-affine instead of affine would suffice, cf. footnote (5), p. .
On this subject see SGA 3 IV, in particular §4.
Cf. loc. cit. in footnote (3), p. .

This notation is coherent with respect to the general cohomological notations (SGA 4 V) only when one has criteria for the effectiveness of descent at one's disposal, which are hardly ensured unless \(G\) is affine (or only quasi-affine, cf. (VIII 9)).

For a systematic
study of this point of view, cf. SGA 4 I to IX.
Cf. J. Giraud, Cohomologie non abélienne, Springer-Verlag 1971.

pages.title: Particular cases of principal bundles
pages.html:
5 Particular cases of principal bundles

Suppose now that \(S\) is connected, and endowed with a geometric point \(a\), whence a fundamental group \(\pi_1(S,a)\) allowing one to classify the étale coverings of \(S\): the category of étale coverings of \(S\) is equivalent to the category of finite sets on which \(\pi_1\) operates continuously. It follows that a finite and étale group-scheme \(G\) over \(S\) is determined essentially by an ordinary finite group \(\mathscr{G}\), on which \(\pi_1\) operates continuously by group automorphisms. An étale covering \(P\) of \(S\) on which \(G\) operates on the right is determined essentially by a finite set \(\mathscr{P}\) on which \(\pi_1\) operates continuously (on the left), and on which \(\mathscr{G}\) operates on the right in a way compatible with the operations of \(\pi_1\):

\[ s(p\cdot g)=(sp)\cdot(sg)\qquad \text{for }s\in\pi_1,\ p\in\mathscr{P},\ g\in\mathscr{G}. \]

One verifies that \(P\) is a principal homogeneous bundle in the sense of 1 if and only if \(\mathscr{P}\) is a principal homogeneous set under \(\mathscr{G}\) (using for example the criterion 2). In other words, the category of principal homogeneous bundles on \(S\) with group \(G\) is equivalent to the category of principal homogeneous bundles with group \(G\) in the category of finite sets on which \(\pi_1\) operates continuously. In particular one deduces from this a canonical bijection, functorial in \(G\):

\[ \tag{*} \mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1,\mathscr{G}), \]

where the second member denotes the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of principal homogeneous bundles under \(\mathscr{G}\) in the category of finite sets on which \(\pi_1\) operates (there is moreover no need to specify: continuously), a set which is made explicit in the well-known way as the quotient set of the set \(Z^1(\pi_1,G)\) of \(1\)-cocycles \(\varphi:\pi_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) (satisfying \(\varphi(1)=1\), \(\varphi(st)=\varphi(s)(s.\varphi(t))\)) by the group \(\mathscr{G}\) which operates on it in a natural way).

An important case is that where \(\pi_1\) operates trivially on \(\mathscr{G}\), i.e. when \(G\) is a completely decomposed covering of \(S\), isomorphic to the sum of \(\mathscr{G}\) copies of \(S\); in that case one also writes \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{G})\) instead of \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\), and this set is in one-to-one correspondence (*) with \(\mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1,\mathscr{G})= \operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1,\mathscr{G})/\)inner automorphisms of \(\mathscr{G}\). One will note moreover that in this case, a principal homogeneous bundle on \(S\) with group \(G\) is nothing other than a principal covering of \(S\) with group \(\mathscr{G}\) (V 7), and the preceding one-to-one correspondence is the one deduced from the correspondence between principal coverings of \(S\) with group \(\mathscr{G}\), pointed above \(a\), and the continuous homomorphisms of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) into \(\mathscr{G}\) (V end of 5).

The interest of relating the theory of étale coverings with that of principal bundles (already implicit in A. Weil, Généralisation des Fonctions Abéliennes, and made explicit by S. Lang in his geometric theory of class fields, cf. Serre [5]), comes from the fact that every \(S\)-group which is finite and étale over \(S\) can be embedded in an \(S\)-group \(H\), affine and smooth over \(S\), with connected fibres, commutative when \(G\) is, so that by the exact sequence 5 (and possibly its non-commutative variants), the “discrete” classification of principal coverings with group \(G\) can be studied by means of the “continuous” classification of principal bundles with group \(H\), and conversely moreover. For the idea of the general construction of the embedding of \(G\) into \(H\) (rather little used in practice it seems), refer to [5]. We content ourselves with developing in what follows two important particular cases, which are moreover classical. We shall need in them an auxiliary result:

Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(G\) an \(S\)-group isomorphic to \(\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n)_S\) (for example \(\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\)) or \(\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\), then every principal homogeneous bundle under \(G\) is locally trivial.

Let us make precise that \(\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n)_S\) (\(n\) an integer \(\geqslant 0\)) denotes the \(S\)-group which represents the contravariant functor \(T\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n,\Gamma(T,\mathscr{O}_T))\) on the \(S\)-prescheme \(T\), in particular \(\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\) (“multiplicative group over \(S\)”) represents the contravariant functor \(T\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \Gamma(T,\mathscr{O}_T^*)\), hence as a prescheme over \(S\) is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Spec} {\mathscr{O}_S[t,t^{-1}]}\), where \(t\) is an indeterminate. Likewise \(\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\) represents the contravariant functor \(T\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \Gamma(T,\mathscr{O}_T)\), it is therefore isomorphic as an \(S\)-prescheme to \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_S[t])\), where \(t\) is an indeterminate. Let us note that by dévissage, 1 recovers the local triviality result of Rosenlicht, relative to the case where \(G\) admits a “composition series” whose consecutive factors are groups of the type considered here. (For a finer study of the questions of local triviality of principal homogeneous bundles, cf. [7] and [3]).

The first assertion is proved by remarking that \(G(T)=\operatorname{Aut}(\mathscr{O}_T^n)\), and that the morphisms \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) intervening in 1 (i.e. which are faithfully flat and quasi-compact) are morphisms of effective descent for the fibred category of Modules locally isomorphic to \(\mathscr{O}_T^n\), i.e. locally free of rank \(n\) (VIII 12). The second is proved in an analogous way, noting that in this case one has \(G(T)=\operatorname{Aut}(\mathscr{E}_T)\), where \(\mathscr{E}_T\) is the trivial extension of \(\mathscr{O}_T\) by \(\mathscr{O}_T\) (and where the automorphisms of course must respect the extension structure), and that the morphisms \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) intervening in 1 are morphisms of effective descent for the fibred category of extensions of \(\mathscr{O}_T\) by \(\mathscr{O}_T\) (as follows easily from VIII 1), and that such extensions are automatically locally trivial.

Remark 2

One will note that the same type of proof applies to the symplectic group \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Sp}}(2n)_S\), taking into account that an alternating form on a module locally isomorphic to \(\mathscr{O}_S^{2n}\), which is “non-degenerate” i.e. defines an isomorphism of this Module onto its dual, is locally isomorphic to the standard form. The result for the orthogonal group is on the contrary false, already if \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, for there can be quadratic forms on a field which are not isomorphic to the standard form. Moreover one shows essentially in [3] that the groups \(\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}\), \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Sp}}\), \(\mathbf{G}_a\) and those which are broken down into such groups, are more or less the only ones for which one has a local triviality result of the type considered here.

Corollary 3

One has canonical bijections

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n)_S)\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n,\mathscr{O}_S)), \]

in particular

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{G}_{m\,S})\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S^*), \]

and

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{G}_{a\,S})\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S), \]

where the second members denote cohomologies of the topological space \(S\) with coefficients in ordinary sheaves.

In particular, \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n)_S)\) is identified with the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of Modules locally free of rank \(n\) on \(S\), and \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{G}_{a\,S})\) is identified with the set of classes of extensions of the Module \(\mathscr{O}_S\) by itself.

blocks.html: 5 Particular cases of principal bundles

Suppose now that \(S\) is connected, and endowed with a geometric point \(a\), whence a fundamental group \(\pi_1(S,a)\) allowing one to classify the étale coverings of \(S\): the category of étale coverings of \(S\) is equivalent to the category of finite sets on which \(\pi_1\) operates continuously. It follows that a finite and étale group-scheme \(G\) over \(S\) is determined essentially by an ordinary finite group \(\mathscr{G}\), on which \(\pi_1\) operates continuously by group automorphisms. An étale covering \(P\) of \(S\) on which \(G\) operates on the right is determined essentially by a finite set \(\mathscr{P}\) on which \(\pi_1\) operates continuously (on the left), and on which \(\mathscr{G}\) operates on the right in a way compatible with the operations of \(\pi_1\):
\[ s(p\cdot g)=(sp)\cdot(sg)\qquad \text{for }s\in\pi_1,\ p\in\mathscr{P},\ g\in\mathscr{G}. \]

One verifies that \(P\) is a principal homogeneous bundle in the sense of 1 if and only if \(\mathscr{P}\) is a principal homogeneous set under \(\mathscr{G}\) (using for example the criterion 2). In other words, the category of principal homogeneous bundles on \(S\) with group \(G\) is equivalent to the category of principal homogeneous bundles with group \(G\) in the category of finite sets on which \(\pi_1\) operates continuously. In particular one deduces from this a canonical bijection, functorial in \(G\):
\[ \tag{*} \mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1,\mathscr{G}), \]

where the second member denotes the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of principal homogeneous bundles under \(\mathscr{G}\) in the category of finite sets on which \(\pi_1\) operates (there is moreover no need to specify: continuously), a set which is made explicit in the well-known way as the quotient set of the set \(Z^1(\pi_1,G)\) of \(1\)-cocycles \(\varphi:\pi_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{G}\) (satisfying \(\varphi(1)=1\), \(\varphi(st)=\varphi(s)(s.\varphi(t))\)) by the group \(\mathscr{G}\) which operates on it in a natural way).

An important case is that where \(\pi_1\) operates trivially on \(\mathscr{G}\), i.e. when \(G\) is a completely decomposed covering of \(S\), isomorphic to the sum of \(\mathscr{G}\) copies of \(S\); in that case one also writes \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{G})\) instead of \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\), and this set is in one-to-one correspondence (*) with \(\mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1,\mathscr{G})= \operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1,\mathscr{G})/\)inner automorphisms of \(\mathscr{G}\). One will note moreover that in this case, a principal homogeneous bundle on \(S\) with group \(G\) is nothing other than a principal covering of \(S\) with group \(\mathscr{G}\) (V 7), and the preceding one-to-one correspondence is the one deduced from the correspondence between principal coverings of \(S\) with group \(\mathscr{G}\), pointed above \(a\), and the continuous homomorphisms of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) into \(\mathscr{G}\) (V end of 5).

The interest of relating the theory of étale coverings with that of principal bundles (already implicit in A. Weil, Généralisation des Fonctions Abéliennes, and made explicit by S. Lang in his geometric theory of class fields, cf. Serre [5]), comes from the fact that every \(S\)-group which is finite and étale over \(S\) can be embedded in an \(S\)-group \(H\), affine and smooth over \(S\), with connected fibres, commutative when \(G\) is, so that by the exact sequence 5 (and possibly its non-commutative variants), the “discrete” classification of principal coverings with group \(G\) can be studied by means of the “continuous” classification of principal bundles with group \(H\), and conversely moreover. For the idea of the general construction of the embedding of \(G\) into \(H\) (rather little used in practice it seems), refer to [5]. We content ourselves with developing in what follows two important particular cases, which are moreover classical. We shall need in them an auxiliary result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(G\) an \(S\)-group isomorphic to \(\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n)_S\) (for example \(\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\)) or \(\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\), then every principal homogeneous bundle under \(G\) is locally trivial.

Let us make precise that \(\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n)_S\) (\(n\) an integer \(\geqslant 0\)) denotes the \(S\)-group which represents the contravariant functor \(T\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n,\Gamma(T,\mathscr{O}_T))\) on the \(S\)-prescheme \(T\), in particular \(\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\) (“multiplicative group over \(S\)”) represents the contravariant functor \(T\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \Gamma(T,\mathscr{O}_T^*)\), hence as a prescheme over \(S\) is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Spec} {\mathscr{O}_S[t,t^{-1}]}\), where \(t\) is an indeterminate. Likewise \(\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\) represents the contravariant functor \(T\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \Gamma(T,\mathscr{O}_T)\), it is therefore isomorphic as an \(S\)-prescheme to \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_S[t])\), where \(t\) is an indeterminate. Let us note that by dévissage, 1 recovers the local triviality result of Rosenlicht, relative to the case where \(G\) admits a “composition series” whose consecutive factors are groups of the type considered here. (For a finer study of the questions of local triviality of principal homogeneous bundles, cf. [7] and [3]).

The first assertion is proved by remarking that \(G(T)=\operatorname{Aut}(\mathscr{O}_T^n)\), and that the morphisms \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) intervening in 1 (i.e. which are faithfully flat and quasi-compact) are morphisms of effective descent for the fibred category of Modules locally isomorphic to \(\mathscr{O}_T^n\), i.e. locally free of rank \(n\) (VIII 12). The second is proved in an analogous way, noting that in this case one has \(G(T)=\operatorname{Aut}(\mathscr{E}_T)\), where \(\mathscr{E}_T\) is the trivial extension of \(\mathscr{O}_T\) by \(\mathscr{O}_T\) (and where the automorphisms of course must respect the extension structure), and that the morphisms \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) intervening in 1 are morphisms of effective descent for the fibred category of extensions of \(\mathscr{O}_T\) by \(\mathscr{O}_T\) (as follows easily from VIII 1), and that such extensions are automatically locally trivial.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2

One will note that the same type of proof applies to the symplectic group \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Sp}}(2n)_S\), taking into account that an alternating form on a module locally isomorphic to \(\mathscr{O}_S^{2n}\), which is “non-degenerate” i.e. defines an isomorphism of this Module onto its dual, is locally isomorphic to the standard form. The result for the orthogonal group is on the contrary false, already if \(S\) is the spectrum of a field, for there can be quadratic forms on a field which are not isomorphic to the standard form. Moreover one shows essentially in [3] that the groups \(\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}\), \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Sp}}\), \(\mathbf{G}_a\) and those which are broken down into such groups, are more or less the only ones for which one has a local triviality result of the type considered here.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

One has canonical bijections

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n)_S)\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n,\mathscr{O}_S)), \]

in particular

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{G}_{m\,S})\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S^*), \]

and

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{G}_{a\,S})\overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S), \]

where the second members denote cohomologies of the topological space \(S\) with coefficients in ordinary sheaves.

In particular, \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\operatorname{\mathbf{GL}}(n)_S)\) is identified with the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of Modules locally free of rank \(n\) on \(S\), and \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{G}_{a\,S})\) is identified with the set of classes of extensions of the Module \(\mathscr{O}_S\) by itself.

pages.title:
Application to principal coverings: theories of
Kummer and Artin-Schreier
pages.html:
6 Application to principal coverings: theories of Kummer and Artin-Schreier

Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(n\) an integer \(>0\), let \(u_n\colon \mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\) be the power homomorphism, and \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\) its kernel. Then \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\) is finite and locally free of rank \(n\) over \(S\), and it is étale over \(S\) if and only if for every \(s\in S\), the characteristic of \(s\) is prime to \(n\). The sequence of homomorphisms

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\xrightarrow{u_n}\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

is exact in the sense of 4. (It will be called the Kummer exact sequence over \(S\), relative to the integer \(n\)).

We have

\[ \mathbf{G}_m=\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_S[t,t^{-1}], \]

and \(u_n\) corresponds to the homomorphism \(u_n\) on the affine \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebras, given by

\[ u_n(t)=t^n, \]

on the other hand the unit section of \(\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\) corresponds to the augmentation homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebras, given by

\[ \varepsilon(t)=1, \]

whose kernel is therefore the principal Ideal \((t-1)\). The image of the latter under \(u_n\) is therefore the principal Ideal \((1-t^n)\), and one finds:

\[ \boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}=\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_S[t]/(1-t^n), \]

which shows in particular that \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\) is finite over \(S\), and defined by an Algebra over \(S\) that is free of rank \(n\), having the basis formed by the \(t^i\) (\(0\leqslant i\leqslant n-1\)). For it to be étale at \(s\in S\), it is necessary and sufficient that the reduced Algebra \(k[t]/(1-t^n)\), where \(k=\mathit{k}(s)\), obtained by formal adjunction of the roots of unity to \(k\), be separable over \(k\), i.e. the roots of \(1-t^n\) in an algebraic closure of \(k\) are all distinct, which is equivalent to the fact that \(n\) is prime to the characteristic. Finally, to show that the sequence of homomorphisms in 1 is exact, one is reduced by virtue of criterion 2 to proving that \(u_n\) is faithfully flat. One may evidently suppose \(S\) affine with ring \(A\), hence \(\mathbf{G}_{m\,S}\) affine with ring \(B=A[t,t^{-1}]\), and it suffices to verify that \(u_n\) makes \(B\) a free module of rank \(n\) over \(B\), or what amounts to the same, that \(u_n\) is injective, and that \(A[t,t^{-1}]\) is a free module of rank \(n\) over \(A[t^n,t^{-n}]\). Indeed, one verifies easily that the \(t^i\) (\(0\leqslant i\leqslant n-1\)) form a basis of the one over the other, which completes the proof.

Definition 2

One calls \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\) the Kummer group of rank \(n\) over \(S\), and one calls a principal Kummerian covering of rank \(n\) over \(S\) any principal homogeneous bundle over \(S\) with group the Kummer group of rank \(n\).

The set of these coverings is a group, denoted \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S})\) or simply \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\). One will note that the formation of the Kummer group of rank \(n\) over \(S\) is compatible with base extension, hence that \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\) arises by base extension from the absolute Kummer group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) over \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbf{Z})\).

Let us denote by \((\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})_S\) the \(S\)-group defined by the ordinary finite group \(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\). If \(G\) is an arbitrary \(S\)-group, the \(S\)-group homomorphisms \(u\) from \((\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})_S\) into \(G\) correspond bijectively, and in a manner compatible with base change, to the sections of \(G\) over \(S\) whose power is the unit section, by making correspond to \(u\) the image under \(u\) of the section of \((\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})_S\) over \(S\) defined by the generator \(1\bmod n\mathbf{Z}\) of \(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\). This being so:

Corollary 3

If \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\) is étale over \(S\), one obtains in this way a bijective correspondence between the isomorphisms of \(S\)-groups \((\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})_S\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\), and the sections of \(\mathscr{O}_S\) that are of order exactly \(n\) on each connected component of \(S\) (such a section will be called a “primitive root of unity over \(S\)”). Hence for \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n\,S}\) to be isomorphic as an \(S\)-group to \((\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})_S\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be étale over \(S\), i.e. that the residue characteristics of \(S\) be prime to \(n\), and that there exist a primitive root of unity over \(S\).

This explains the role played in classical Kummer theory by the hypothesis that the base field (playing the role of \(S\)) be of characteristic prime to \(n\) and contain the roots of unity, and by the choice of a primitive root of unity. Once one has the language of schemes at one's disposal, there is no longer any reason to encumber oneself with these hypotheses, and it is appropriate to reason directly on \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) instead of \(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\). Thus, the conjunction of 1, 5 and 3 gives us the following general relation between the theory of principal Kummerian coverings and that of Picard groups:

Proposition 4

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, one has a canonical exact sequence

\[ \textstyle 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}^*_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}^*_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}^*_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}^*_S), \]

whence, setting \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S^*)=\operatorname{Pic}(S)\), and denoting for every abelian group \(A\), by \({_n}A\) and \(A_n\) the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by \(n\) in \(A\), the exact sequence:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^*_n\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {{}_n\operatorname{Pic}}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0. \]

We shall make explicit two important cases, where one or the other extreme term of this exact sequence is zero:

Corollary 5

Suppose \({}_n\operatorname{Pic}(S)=0\), (for example that \(S\) is the spectrum of a local ring, or of a factorial ring), and let \(A\) be the ring \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\). Then one has a canonical isomorphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} A^*/{A^*}^n. \]

This is essentially the classical statement of Kummer theory, when \(S\) is the spectrum of a field.

Corollary 6

Suppose that every element of \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\) is a power, for example that \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\) is a composite of algebraically closed fields or that \(S\) is reduced and proper over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Then one has a canonical isomorphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}{}_n\operatorname{Pic}(S). \]

In particular, when \(S\) is proper and connected over an algebraically closed field \(k\), this puts into relation the fundamental group of \(S\) with the points of finite order of the Picard scheme \(P\) of \(S\) over \(k\); thus one will have an isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1(S),\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z})\simeq {_n P}(k) \]

for \(n\) prime to the characteristic, which is often used in algebraic geometry. As an application, when the connected component \(P^0\) of \(P\) is a complete group scheme, of dimension \(g\), one sees, using the results recalled in 2, and the finiteness of the torsion group of Néron-Severi, that for every prime number \(\ell\) prime to the characteristic, the \(\ell\)-primary component of the fundamental group \(\pi_1(S)\) made abelian is a module of finite type and of rank \(2g\) over the ring \(\mathbf{Z}_\ell\) of \(\ell\)-adic integers (and moreover free except for at most a finite number of values of \(\ell\)). As Serre remarked, this makes it possible to prove under certain conditions that when \(X\) is a flat and projective scheme over \(S\) connected, then the Picard schemes of the fibres of \(X\) all have the same dimension, by applying the semicontinuity theorem (X 3); Serre's argument applies as soon as the Picard scheme of \(X\) over \(S\) exists, and the connected Picards of the fibres of \(X\) over \(S\) are proper group schemes, for example when the geometric fibres of \(X\) over \(S\) are normal (\(X\) being always flat and projective over \(S\)), in particular if \(X\) is smooth and projective over \(S\).

Let now \(p\) be a prime number, and suppose that \(S\) is a prescheme of characteristic \(p\), i.e. such that \(p\cdot\mathscr{O}_S=0\). Then the power homomorphism in \(\mathscr{O}_S\) is additive, and the corresponding morphism, obtained by replacing \(S\) by a variable \(T\) over \(S\):

\[ F\colon\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{G}_{a\,S} \]

is therefore a homomorphism of \(S\)-groups, which one calls the Frobenius homomorphism (N.B. Such a morphism is defined for every \(S\)-prescheme \(G\) which comes by base extension from a prescheme \(G_0\) over the prime field \(\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}\), and this morphism is a group homomorphism if \(G_0\) is a group prescheme). We shall set:

\[ \wp=\mathrm{id}-F\colon\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}. \]

Consider on the other hand the \(S\)-group \((\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})_S\) defined by the ordinary finite group \(\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}\); we have said that for every \(S\)-group \(G\), the \(S\)-group homomorphisms from \((\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})_S\) into \(G\) correspond biunivocally to the sections of \(G\) over \(S\) whose power is the unit section. When \(G=\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\), they correspond therefore to the arbitrary sections of \(G\) over \(S\). Taking in particular the section of \(\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\) over \(S\) corresponding to the unit section of the sheaf of rings \(\mathscr{O}_S\), one finds a homomorphism of \(S\)-groups

\[ i\colon(\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{G}_{a\,S} \]
Proposition 7

The sequence of homomorphisms of \(S\)-groups

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}(\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})_S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{G}_{a\,S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

is exact (in the sense of 4). (One calls it the exact Artin-Schreier sequence over \(S\)).

It suffices to prove it over the prime field \(k=\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}\). It suffices to remark that the homomorphism \(\wp^*\colon k[t]\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k[t]\) defined by \(\wp^*(t)=t-t^p\) makes \(k[t]\) a free module of rank \(p\) over \(k[t]\), more precisely that \(k[t]\) is a free module over \(k[s]\), where \(s=t-t^p\), having the basis formed by the \(t^i\) (\(0\leqslant i\leqslant p-1\)).

One concludes from this, using 5 and 3:

Proposition 8

One has a canonical exact sequence:

\begin{multline*} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})\\ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S), \end{multline*}

whence an exact sequence:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)/\wp\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

(where the exponent \(F\) in the last term means the subgroup of invariants under the endomorphism \(F\), equal to the kernel of \(\wp=\mathrm{id}-F\)).

Let us make explicit two more extreme cases:

Corollary 9

Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^F=0\), for example that \(S\) is an affine scheme. Then, setting \(A=\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\), one has a canonical isomorphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} A/\wp A. \]

This is the Artin-Schreier theory in the classical form, at least when \(A\) is the spectrum of a field.

Corollary 10

Suppose that \(\wp\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S){=}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\), for example that \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\) is a composite of algebraically closed fields, or that \(S\) is proper over an algebraically closed field. Then one has a canonical isomorphism:

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^F \]
Remarks 11

The last statement is due to J-P. Serre [9]. It is also possible to develop an analogous theory for the structural group \(\mathbf{Z}/p^n\mathbf{Z}\) for arbitrary \(n\), using in place of \(\mathbf{G}_a\) the Witt group scheme \(\mathbf{W}_n\), cf. loc. cit. One notes that in characteristic \(p>0\), the theory of Kummer no longer gives any information on the principal coverings of order \(p\), since \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) is then an “infinitesimal” group i.e. radicial over the base, hence without direct relation with \(\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}\); thus at first sight, the theory of these coverings is no longer amenable (when \(S\) is a proper scheme over an algebraically closed field, to fix ideas) to the theory of the Picard scheme as in 6. Nevertheless, if one recalls that the Zariski tangent space at the origin in \(\mathbf{Pic}_{S/K}\)1 is identified with \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\), one observes that the knowledge of the group scheme \({_p}\mathbf{Pic}_{S/k}\), the kernel of multiplication by \(p\) in \(\mathbf{Pic}_{S/k}\), implies that of \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})\) as well as that of \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\mu}_p)\); one notes that it also implies that of \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)\), where \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) denotes the infinitesimal group scheme over the prime field, the kernel of \(F\colon\mathbf{G}_a\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{G}_a\) (which can also be described as the spectrum of the restricted enveloping algebra of the trivial \(p\)-Lie algebra of dimension \(1\)): indeed the exact sequence 5 gives here:

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)\simeq\operatorname{Ker} (F\colon\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)), \]

and more generally, denoting by \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^n}\) the kernel in \(\mathbf{G}_a\) of the iterate of \(F\), one will have

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(S,\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^n})\simeq\operatorname{Ker}(F^n\colon\mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(S,\mathscr{O}_S)). \]

In fact, the knowledge of \({_p}\mathbf{Pic}_{S/k}\) is equivalent to that of \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) for every finite commutative algebraic group annihilated by \(p\); more generally, the knowledge of \({_{p^n}}\mathbf{Pic}_{S/k}\) is equivalent to that of \(\mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\) for every finite commutative algebraic group \(G\) annihilated by \(p^n\), by virtue of the following theorem which encompasses, in the case considered, both the Kummer theory and that of Artin-Schreier:

Let \(G\) be a finite algebraic group over \(k\), \(D(G)=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{k\textup{-groupes}}(G,\mathbf{G}_m)\) its Cartier dual (whose affine algebra is carried by the dual vector space of the affine algebra of \(G\), i.e. by the hyperalgebra of \(G\) in the sense of Dieudonné-Cartier); then one has a canonical isomorphism:

\[ \tag{*} \mathrm{H}^1(S,G)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}_{k\textup{-groupes}}(D(G),\mathbf{Pic}_{S/k}). \]

(N.B. \(S\) is a proper scheme over \(k\) algebraically closed, such that \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathscr{O}_S)=k\)). This formula can still be expressed by saying that the “true fundamental group” of \(S\) alluded to in 2, made abelian, is isomorphic to the inverse limit of the \(D(P_i)\), where \(P_i\) runs through the finite algebraic subgroups of \(\mathbf{Pic}_{S/k}\), which will be denoted \(T^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}(\mathbf{Pic}_{X/k})\). When \(S\) is an abelian variety, one has seen in 1 that this group is also isomorphic to the “true” Tate module \(T_{{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}}(S)=\varprojlim\, {{_n}S}\), and the isomorphism (*) is then written in a more striking way

\[ \operatorname{Ext}^1(A,G)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(D(G),B), \]

\(A\) being an abelian variety, \(B\) its dual, \(G\) a finite algebraic group over \(k\). The results which have just been indicated can moreover be generalized to the case where \(k\) is replaced by an arbitrary base prescheme, and to coefficient groups \(G\) other than finite groups.
